title: Indentation and Blocks
summary: Understand why indentation controls structure in Python.

sketchBlocks.title: Understanding Indentation
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
In Python, indentation is not just for readability; it defines the structure of your code. For example:

```python
if condition:
    # This block is executed if the condition is True
    print('Condition is True')
else:
    # This block is executed if the condition is False
    print('Condition is False')
```

In this example, the indented lines under `if` and `else` are part of their respective blocks. If you forget to indent, Python will raise an `IndentationError`. 

**Your turn:** Try writing your own `if` statement with an `else` block!

sketchBlocks.title: Nested Indentation

quizDraft.title: Identifying Indentation Errors
quizDraft.prompt: Which of the following code snippets will raise an IndentationError?
options:
if x > 10:
print('x is greater than 10')

if x > 10:
    print('x is greater than 10')
quizDraft.hint: Check the indentation levels in the snippets.

help.concept: Indentation in Python defines the blocks of code. Incorrect indentation can lead to errors.
help.hint_1: Look for lines that are not properly indented under their respective conditions.
help.hint_2: Remember that all lines in a block must be indented the same amount.

quizDraft.title: Complete the Indentation
quizDraft.prompt:
Fill in the blank to correctly complete the if statement:

```python
if x > 5:
    _____
```
choices: print('x is less than or equal to 5')

quizDraft.title: Indentation Practice
quizDraft.prompt: Write a function that checks if a number is positive, negative, or zero. Use proper indentation.

quizDraft.title: Nested Conditions
quizDraft.prompt: Create a function that checks if a number is even or odd and greater than 10. Use proper indentation.

quizDraft.title: Multiple Conditions
quizDraft.prompt: Write a function that checks if a number is positive, negative, or zero, and also checks if it's even or odd.
